ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried tofu class (FOODON_03302757); no Thai-style variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried tofu generically; no Thai-style sub-category exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Thai-style fried tofu. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Thai Style Fried Tofu is approximately 200 g, comprising several pieces of deep-fried tofu accompanied by a Thai-style sweet chilli dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fried tofu (firm, prepared with oil) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 16301 / FDC ID 174284
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for firm tofu, fried (NDB 16301 / FDC 174284) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values represent plain deep-fried firm tofu per 100 g. Thai-style seasoning (chilli sauce, fish sauce, lime) contributes minor additional sodium and sugar; sodium adjusted upward by approximately 80 mg/100 g to reflect typical Thai dipping sauce contribution at serving ratio. All other macros retained from USDA source. Estimation basis documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Tofu, plain (soybean curd) — Glycemic Index entry; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style fried tofu. GI of 15 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain tofu / soybean curd (GI ~15, low category), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. Deep-frying does not substantially alter the GI of tofu given its low carbohydrate content. Thai chilli dipping sauce adds minor sugar but at typical serving ratios does not elevate the composite GI above the low category. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 13 g) / 100 ≈ 2.0 (serving 200 g, carbohydrate ~6.5 g/100 g = 13 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving of Thai Style Fried Tofu contributes approximately 680 mg sodium, representing about 30% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce portions or substitute low-sodium soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of ~2 per serving, Thai Style Fried Tofu has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose, and its high protein content (17 g/100 g) supports satiety; however, the sweet chilli dipping sauce adds free sugars and should be used sparingly by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fat profile with low saturated fat and soy isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tofu is rich in polyunsaturated fatty acids (11 g/100 g) and soy isoflavones, which are associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction; saturated fat is low at 2.9 g/100 g, making this dish a heart-friendly protein choice when consumed without excess dipping sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Style Fried Tofu (泰式炸豆腐) is a popular dish served in Malaysian and Singaporean food courts and restaurants, particularly at Yong Tau Foo stalls. Firm tofu blocks are deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains soft and silken. The dish is typically served with a Thai-inspired sweet chilli dipping sauce made from red chillies, garlic, lime juice, sugar, and fish sauce or soy sauce, and garnished with fresh coriander. The flavour profile combines the mild, nutty taste of soy with the bright, tangy heat of the Thai sauce. Nutritionally, fried tofu is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with a low glycemic index (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Each 100 g provides approximately 271 kcal, 17 g protein, 19.5 g fat (predominantly polyunsaturated), and 6.5 g carbohydrate. Tofu is an excellent source of calcium (372 mg/100 g) and iron (3.4 mg/100 g), and contains soy isoflavones with potential cardiovascular benefits. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from the dipping sauce, which can be significant for hypertensive individuals. This dish is suitable for vegetarians when prepared with soy sauce instead of fish sauce, and is naturally gluten-free when tamari is used. It is commonly ordered as a side dish or protein component in a mixed Yong Tau Foo meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai fried tofu?
Is fried tofu good for diabetics?
Is Thai tofu high in protein?
Can hypertensive patients eat Thai fried tofu?
Thai style tofu GI glycemic index

vegetarian protein option (when fish sauce omitted)

sweet chilli sauce: น้ำจิ้มไก่

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai-style fried tofu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for firm fried tofu (FDC 174284), which is a well-validated source for plain fried tofu. Sodium has been adjusted upward to account for typical Thai dipping sauce contribution. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain tofu. Confidence is medium rather than high because the dish-level composite (tofu plus Thai sauce) has not been directly measured in an approved primary regional source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 174284 (firm tofu, fried) with sodium adjusted upward by ~80 mg/100 g to reflect typical Thai sweet chilli dipping sauce contribution at a 200 g serving with approximately 30 g sauce. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for vegetable-oil-fried tofu; no partially hydrogenated oils reported. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; tofu is a plant-based food containing no dietary cholesterol. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; tofu is a plant-based food with no natural B12. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. 8. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (plain tofu); no direct measurement for Thai-style fried tofu exists in approved sources. 9. Halal status is marked false because fish sauce (non-halal if not certified) is a common ingredient; halal-certified variants exist but cannot be confirmed for this field survey listing. 10. The input food_name '泰式炸豆腐' has been preserved in chinese_simplified and chinese_traditional local_names fields. 11. Cuisine is retained as Malaysian per input, reflecting the Singaporean-Malaysian hawker context of this Thai-influenced dish.
